results.question: If a unit expends 1 MF/MP and is attacked, may it be attacked on that same MF/MP by a different weapon or by its inherent firepower? For example, a squad fails an initial PIAT/PF check and sacrifices its inherent FP to attempt a second PF check; if the second PF check succeeds, may that PF fire at the same target on that same 1 MP expenditure?
results.expected_answer: Yes to both. This is settled by the official A8.14 Q&A: when a unit expends 1 MF/MP and is attacked, on that same MF/MP it may also be attacked by a different weapon and/or by its inherent FP wherever Mandatory Fire Group does not apply. So a squad that fails an initial PF check may sacrifice its inherent FP, make a second PF check, and—if it passes—fire that PF at the same target on that same 1 MP expenditure. Likewise a squad possessing two weapons not subject to Mandatory FG (e.g., two PF) may fire both on that single MF/MP.
results.model_response:
**Answer:** Yes. A single 1 MF/MP expenditure may be attacked separately by different weapons and/or a unit's inherent FP (provided Mandatory Fire Group requirements do not apply), and a squad may even attempt to fire two PF on that same one-point expenditure (A8.14 Q&A). In your example, since a PF Check may be made during a Defensive First Fire opportunity and a successful check "must be immediately fired" (C13.31), the second PF — gained at the cost of the squad's inherent FP for the phase (A7.351) — fires at the target on that same 1 MP expenditure.

---

References:
- (A8.1) Defensive First Fire — when a unit/stack expends MF/MP in LOS, the DEFENDER may halt its movement and fire at it "with as many attacks as he can bring to bear" in that Location.
- (A8.14) Q&A — a unit that expends 1 MF/MP and is attacked may be attacked on that same MF/MP by a different weapon or by the firer's inherent FP; a squad may fire two SW (e.g., two PF) on the same one-point MF/MP expenditure. [Yes to both]
- (A9.61 & C13.31) Q&A — a German squad may attempt to fire two PF when an enemy unit expends only one MP/MF. [Yes]
- (C13.31) PF Usage — a PF Check dr may be made during a friendly fire phase (including a Defensive First Fire opportunity during the enemy MPh) and, if successful, the PF must be immediately fired; provided the squad has not yet fired its inherent FP, it can attempt a second PF in the same phase, but that constitutes use of two SW and costs the squad its inherent FP for that phase.
- (A7.351) Using two SW causes a squad to forfeit its inherent FP for that phase.
results.llm_reasoning: [Explanation] The model's answer matches the expected answer: it affirms both parts (yes to different weapon and yes to inherent FP), cites the A8.14 Q&A, notes the Mandatory Fire Group caveat, and correctly explains the PF-check/sacrifice-of-inherent-FP sequence and that using two SW forfeits inherent FP (A7.351/C13.31). The response covers the key concepts and procedural outcome described in the expected answer.

results.question: When a squad spends 1 MF in the MPh to place SMOKE grenades in its own hex and succeeds, is any announced Defensive First Fire (DFF) during that 1 MF expenditure resolved with the +2 SMOKE DRM or without it? In other words, is the DFF shot resolved before or after the SMOKE counter is placed?
results.expected_answer: After the SMOKE is placed—so the +2 SMOKE DRM applies to the Defensive First Fire. Per the official A8.1/A24.1 Q&A, the SMOKE-placement MF expenditure, the placement dr, and (if successful) the actual placement of the SMOKE counter are all completed before any DFF is resolved. The placing unit always gets its placement attempt; SMOKE placement is not interrupted by DFF (only DC placement can be interrupted). The DFF triggered by that 1 MF is therefore resolved against a unit already in its own SMOKE and receives the +2 DRM.
results.model_response:
**Answer:** The DFF shot is resolved *after* the SMOKE counter is placed, so it is resolved *with* the +2 SMOKE Hindrance DRM. Per official Q&A on A8. & A24.1, the SMOKE Placement MF expenditure, the SMOKE placement dr, and (if successful) the actual placement of the SMOKE counter are all completed before any Defensive First Fire is resolved.

---

The Defender may still declare Defensive First Fire against the 1 MF expenditure, but that fire cannot "beat" the smoke: the placement attempt is fully resolved first, and only then is the DFF attack made — through the newly placed SMOKE, adding its +2 Hindrance DRM (A24.2) to the attack. Consequently, pinning, breaking, or even eliminating the placing squad with that DFF shot cannot retroactively prevent the SMOKE placement — the counter is already on the map.

This is consistent with the general timing principle for MF expenditures that allow an activity: Residual FP (and by extension First Fire triggered by the expenditure) does not attack until the activity the MF expenditure allowed is completed — placing SMOKE grenades is explicitly listed as such an activity (A8.221 notes).

References:
- (A24.1) Infantry SMOKE Usage — placing SMOKE grenades in the squad's own Location costs 1 MF; the hex must be specified before rolling the placement dr
- (A8. & A24.1 Q&A) The SMOKE Placement MF expenditure, placement dr, and (if successful) counter placement are all done before any Defensive First Fire
- (A8.221) Residual FP does not attack until any activity the MF/MP expenditure allowed is completed (placing SMOKE grenades is given as an example)
- (A24.2) SMOKE Effect — all Direct Fire traced into, through, within, or out of a SMOKE Location suffers the SMOKE Hindrance DRM (+2 for Smoke grenades)
- (A24.11) The 1/2" SMOKE counter remains until the end of the ATTACKER's MPh, after all opposing First Fire but before Defensive Final Fire
results.llm_reasoning: [Explanation] The model's response matches the expected answer: it clearly states the DFF is resolved after the SMOKE counter is placed and thus the +2 SMOKE DRM applies. It cites the relevant timing principles and Q&A (A8.1/A24.1) and notes that SMOKE placement cannot be interrupted by DFF, which are the key points required for a correct explanation.

results.question: A11.8 requires a vehicle to be on a road and adjacent to building hexes on both sides to be subject to CC Reaction fire and street fighting. If one of those adjacent building hexes is rubble (B24.1 says a rubble location is no longer a building location), does that rubble hex still meet the requirement for street fighting?
results.expected_answer: By the standard rules, no. A rubbled hex is no longer a building Location (B24.1) and is not a 'building hex' for A11.8 Street Fighting, so a vehicle that is adjacent to a rubble hex (rather than a building hex) on that side does not meet the requirement to be flanked by building hexes on both sides. This is overridden only when a Special Scenario Rule states that rubble still counts as a building hex for Street Fighting (e.g., RF8 in Red Factories)—and the very need for such SSRs confirms that rubble does not count by default.
results.model_response:
**Answer:** No. B24.1 states that "a rubbled Location is no longer a building Location," so a rubble hex does not count as one of the required adjacent building hexes for Street Fighting/CC Reaction Fire (A11.8). Unless a scenario SSR says otherwise, the vehicle in that road hex is not subject to Street Fighting.

---

A11.8 makes a vehicle "especially vulnerable to CC" only if it is in a road hex ADJACENT to a building hex on both sides of that road, and the attacking Infantry must occupy the ground level of one of those adjacent building hexes. Reading the rule literally, both flanking hexes must actually be building hexes at the time of the attack. Once a hex is rubbled, B24.1 removes its status as a building Location — it is rubble terrain, not a building — so the "building hex on both sides" condition fails.

Supporting evidence that this is the intended reading: in Valor of the Guards, SSR VotG8 had to state that "Stone rubble is treated as a building for Rout, Rally, Ambush and Street Fighting purposes" — a special rule that would be unnecessary if rubble already qualified as a building for A11.8 under the standard rules (and even under that SSR, official Q&A confirms the rubble is still not part of the same building).

Do not confuse this with the separate exception for rubble ON the road itself: a road covered by rubble/debris is treated as non-existent, EXC: for Street Fighting (A11.8) purposes (B/O1.2, RB O3.43). That exception preserves the road's status for Street Fighting when the road hex is rubbled — it says nothing about letting a rubbled flanking hex count as a building.

References:
- (A11.8) STREET FIGHTING — vehicle must be in a road hex ADJACENT to a building hex on both sides; attackers advance/react from the ground level of those adjacent building hexes
- (B24.1) "A rubbled Location is no longer a building Location" (also confirmed in official Q&A regarding building control)
- (A10.51 & VotG SSR Q&A) "Stone rubble is treated as a building for ... Street Fighting purposes" exists only as an SSR, implying rubble does not qualify under standard rules
- (O1.2/O3.43) A road covered by rubble/debris is non-existent [EXC: for Street Fighting (A11.8) purposes] — an exception for the road hex itself, not for the adjacent building requirement
results.llm_reasoning: [Explanation] The model's response matches the expected answer: it correctly states that rubbled hexes are not building Locations under B24.1 and therefore do not satisfy A11.8's requirement for flanking building hexes, notes that only an SSR can override this (with VotG as an example), and clarifies the separate exception that applies to rubble covering the road itself. It includes the key concepts and supporting references from the expected answer, so it is correct.

results.question: Is a leader (e.g., a single non-heroic SMC) allowed to destroy a captured gun, or is there a rule prohibiting it?
results.expected_answer: By the rules as written, no. A unit may destroy a weapon only if it could fire it (A9.73), and a single non-heroic SMC cannot fire most Guns (A21.13)—so a lone non-heroic leader cannot destroy such a captured Gun. (This exact situation has not been addressed by official Q&A; many expect it would be allowed if asked, and some permit it as a house rule, but strictly per A9.73/A21.13 the leader may not.)
results.model_response:
**Answer:** Yes — a lone non-heroic leader may destroy a captured Gun; there is no rule prohibiting it. Gun destruction (A9.73) only requires a Good Order Infantry unit in possession of the Gun, and an SMC qualifies as an Infantry unit that can possess a captured Gun. The captured-use and non-qualified-use penalties (A21.11-.13) apply to *firing* the weapon, not to destroying it.

---

The key distinction is between *using* (firing) a crewed weapon and merely *possessing/destroying* it:

- Firing a Gun normally requires a qualified crew, and non-qualified units (including leaders) suffer the Captured Use / non-qualified use penalties of A21.11-A21.13. The rules explicitly contemplate SMCs and leaders operating crewed weapons in this penalized fashion — e.g., a hero "may fire any Gun ≤ 82mm that normally requires a crew as if it were captured and being fired by an enemy crew (21.11 & 21.12)" (A15.23), and the MOL-Projector rule notes "a Russian non-heroic leader may use one with non-qualified use (A21.13) penalties." So a leader being a single man is not, by itself, a bar to interacting with a crewed weapon — it only imposes DRM penalties when he fires it.

- Destroying a Gun (A9.73) is a voluntary elimination of a weapon in the unit's possession, not a fire attack, so no To Hit/IFT penalty structure is involved and no crew requirement applies. Read literally, nothing in A9.73 or A21.13 restricts the destruction to MMCs or qualified crews; any Good Order Infantry unit possessing the captured Gun — including a lone leader — may do it.

Practical caveat: the leader must be in Good Order (unbroken, unpinned, not berserk/in Melee) and must actually possess the captured Gun, and the destruction is performed at the time/phase specified by A9.73.

References:
- (A9.73) Gun destruction — voluntary elimination of a possessed Gun by a Good Order Infantry unit; no crew/MMC requirement stated
- (A21.13) Non-qualified use — imposes firing penalties on non-crew users (leaders included); it penalizes use, it does not prohibit possession or destruction
- (A15.23) A hero may fire a crew-served Gun ≤ 82mm as if captured (21.11 & 21.12) — showing SMCs may operate Guns with penalties
- (A21.11-.12) Captured Use penalties — apply to TH/IFT attempts with the captured weapon, not to its disposal
results.llm_reasoning: [Explanation] The model's answer contradicts the rules-as-written. A9.73 allows a unit to voluntarily destroy a possessed gun only if the unit could fire that gun; it does not permit destruction by any possessor irrespective of firing capability. A21.13 (and related captured-use rules A21.11-.12) mean a single non-heroic SMC/leader cannot legally fire most crew-served guns, so such a lone leader also may not meet the "could fire" requirement of A9.73 and therefore may not destroy the captured gun. The model incorrectly claimed destruction is allowed without the ability to fire and misapplied the captured-use rules (which govern firing penalties) to justify destruction; that reasoning is inconsistent with A9.73/A21.13.

results.question: Does Height Advantage (HA) prevent loss of Concealment when a unit is advancing? For example, if a unit advances into an Open Ground hex but a theoretical shot at it would receive the HA TEM, does it keep Concealment? Are there exceptions while advancing?
results.expected_answer: Yes—Height Advantage can preserve Concealment. Under A10.531 a hex counts as Open Ground (for '?' loss, rout, Dash, Interdiction) only if no effective protective TEM or LOS Hindrance would apply to a shot at the unit; if a theoretical shot would receive the Height Advantage TEM, the unit keeps its '?'. The key exception when advancing is B10.31: a unit advancing from lower to higher elevation across a crestline into an Open Ground hex does NOT get HA against a shooter who has LOS across the very hexside being crossed—so HA does not apply and the advancing unit loses Concealment in that case. Rule of thumb: if FFMO would be cancelled for anyone shooting at you (while assault-moving/advancing), you keep Concealment.
results.model_response:
**Answer:** Yes. A hex is "Open Ground" for concealment-loss purposes (A10.531) only if a hypothetical shot at the unit would qualify for FFMO with no positive DRM — and the +1 Height Advantage TEM (B10.31) is such a positive DRM. So if every enemy unit with LOS is at a lower elevation and the theoretical shot would receive the HA TEM, the advancing unit keeps its "?". This is confirmed by official Q&A: a concealed unit advancing into an open-ground hill hex seen only by a lower-level enemy does NOT lose concealment.

---

How it works:
- A concealed unit loses its "?" when it moves/advances/withdraws into an Open Ground hex "as defined in 10.531" in LOS of a Good Order enemy ground unit within 16 hexes (A12.14). Open Ground per A10.531 is evaluated by asking whether a hypothetical Defensive First Fire shot would get FFMO/no positive DRM.
- B10.31 grants a +1 HA TEM to a target at higher elevation than the firer (if the target hex has no other TEM), and HA negates FFMO/Interdiction. Any elevation difference suffices, per Q&A (e.g., level-1 hill vs. lower firer, upslope 3/4-level hexsides).
- Official Q&A (A10.531): concealed unit advances into an OG hill hex devoid of cover; the only enemy with LOS is at a lower Location — "Does the advancing unit lose concealment? A. No." The same logic is applied to wall TEM (A12.14 Q&A: wall TEM prevents concealment loss while advancing in otherwise open ground).

Exceptions/caveats while advancing:
- The test is per enemy unit: if ANY Good Order enemy within 16 hexes at equal or higher elevation has a true Open Ground LOS to the hex, concealment is lost (cf. B10.31 Q&A — the unit advancing AA2-to-BB1 loses "?" to the unit in Z1, which gets FFMO).
- HA applies only if the target hex has no other TEM ("+1 HA TEM if no other TEM," Terrain Chart note), and HA is NA in certain terrain per the Terrain Chart notes.
- Rooftops: a unit on a rooftop gets the +1 HA DRM but "is considered to be in Open Ground for FFMO, Rout, and Interdiction purposes from equal or higher LOS" (B23.81), with special concealment restrictions (B23.82) — so HA does not protect there against equal/higher viewers.
- Vehicles, Cavalry, and non-Emplaced Guns are different: they lose "?" whenever they move in LOS of a Good Order enemy ground unit within 16 hexes regardless of terrain (A12.2), so HA does not help them.

References:
- (A10.531) Open Ground definition — "more about just what is OG for both rout and non-rout purposes"; positive DRM prevents the hex from being Open Ground (per Q&A with A10.532)
- (A12.14) "?" lost when a concealed unit moves/advances into an Open Ground hex (as defined in 10.531) in LOS within 16 hexes
- (B10.31) Height Advantage +1 TEM for a target higher than the firer; negates FFMO/Interdiction (Q&A: any elevation difference; can apply even on woods-roads per B13.31 Q&A)
- (A10.531 Q&A) Advancing concealed unit seen only from lower level keeps concealment
- (B23.81) Rooftop exception — HA DRM applies but the Location is Open Ground for FFMO/Rout/Interdiction from equal or higher LOS
- (A12.2) Concealed vehicles/Guns lose "?" on any movement in LOS within 16 hexes, regardless of terrain
results.llm_reasoning: [Explanation] The model's answer correctly states the core rule: Height Advantage is a +1 TEM that can prevent a hex from being "Open Ground" for concealment-loss purposes (A10.531) because it cancels FFMO/Interdiction, and the answer properly notes the per-enemy-unit test and other relevant exceptions (rooftops, vehicles/guns, requirement that HA applies only if no other TEM). However, it fails to clearly and explicitly state the important advancing exception from the expected answer: when advancing from a lower to a higher elevation across a crestline (i.e., LOS across the hexside being crossed), the advancing unit does NOT get HA against a shooter who has LOS across that hexside and thus would lose concealment. The model's wording (saying "any elevation difference suffices" and emphasizing HA applicability) could be read as overlooking that specific crestline/hexside crossing exception, which was a key point in the expected answer. Because most key concepts are present but that specific exception is missing/ambiguous, the judgement is Partial.

results.question: If a multi-hex FG cowers, is Random Selection applied across all individual units (and their side weapons) to determine which unit(s) are marked with a Prep or Final Fire counter, or are hex(es) randomly selected first and then units within those hexes chosen?
results.expected_answer: Random Selection is applied directly to the individual units (and their SW) to determine which become marked with a Prep or Final Fire counter. A7.9: 'If a FG cowers, Random Selection is used to determine the unit(s) (and its SW) that becomes marked with a Prep or Final Fire counter.' Hexes are not selected first; the multi-hex composition of the FG is irrelevant to the selection.
results.model_response:
**Answer:** Random Selection is applied directly across all the individual units of the FG — there is no intermediate step of randomly selecting a hex first. Per A7.9, "if a FG cowers, Random Selection is used to determine the unit(s) (and its SW) which becomes marked with a Prep or Final Fire counter," and that selection is made among the units themselves (excluding any units immune to Cowering), with each selected unit's SW marked along with it.

---

The rule and the published Q&A treat the FG's units as the pool for Random Selection, regardless of how many hexes the FG occupies:

- A7.9 states that when a FG cowers, Random Selection determines "the unit(s) (and its SW)" marked with a Prep or Final Fire counter — the selection is by unit, and a selected unit's Support Weapons are marked with it automatically rather than being selected separately.
- The A7.9 Q&A on mixed FGs confirms the selection pool is the units: when a FG contains units immune to Cowering (e.g., British Elite/1st Line, SMC, berserk/Fanatic units, Finns [EXC: Conscripts]) and units that are not, the attack still cowers, and "Random Selection to determine which units cowered is applied only among units not immune to cowering."
- The A7.9/A9.22 Q&A (two squads firing as a FG, Random Selection designating one squad to be marked with Final Fire) likewise shows Random Selection operating directly on the units of the FG, with the non-selected unit unaffected.

Nothing in A7.9 or its Q&A calls for randomly selecting hexes/Locations first; a hex-based procedure would also skew the odds against units stacked alone, which is not supported by the rule text.

References:
- (A7.9) COWERING — if a FG cowers, Random Selection determines the unit(s) (and its SW) marked with a Prep/Final Fire counter; lists units immune to Cowering (SMC, berserk/Fanatic, British Elite and 1st Line, Finns [EXC: Conscripts (A25.7)], etc.)
- (A7.9 Q&A) Mixed FG with cower-immune units — Random Selection applied only among units not immune to Cowering
- (A7.9/A9.22 Q&A) FG of two squads cowers — Random Selection designates one squad to be marked with Final Fire, illustrating unit-by-unit selection
results.llm_reasoning: [Explanation] The model's response matches the expected answer: it states Random Selection is applied to individual units (and their SW) rather than selecting hexes first, notes that units immune to cowering are excluded from the selection pool, and explicitly says the multi-hex composition is irrelevant. These are the key points in the expected answer, so the response is correct.

results.question: All open ground. A squad begins its move in the same hex as an AFV and is moving with that AFV as an Armored Assault. May the squad move 4 MF with the AFV, then declare a late Double Time (late CX) while remaining in their hex (i.e., not actually moving further) so that the AFV may move one additional hex?
results.expected_answer: Yes. The squad may declare late Double Time while in its hex so the AFV it is making the Armored Assault with can advance one additional hex. An official Q&A requires the infantry to actually declare the late Double Time; but, just as a unit declaring Double Time early need not spend its two extra MF, the squad here need not expend the MF it gains—declaring it is enough to let the AFV move the extra hex (the AFV can never move farther than the accompanying infantry could). The infantry does nothing else and stops.
results.model_response:
**Answer:** Yes. Double Time may now be declared at any point during a unit's MPh — but a late declaration (after MF have been expended) yields only *one* extra MF, not two (A4.5). That one extra MF raises the squad's potential MF to 5, and since an Armored Assault AFV may move as far as it could have "if accompanied by that same Infantry throughout the move — even if that Infantry fails to end its MPh in the same hex" (D9.31), the AFV may move one additional Open Ground hex. The squad does not have to actually spend the MF; it does become CX with all CX penalties (A4.51).

---

The D9.31 Armored Assault example covers this case almost verbatim: with the squad and Tiger having expended MF together, "had the squad declared Double Time upon entering [the hex] to gain one extra MF, the Tiger could get to R1 but no farther" — i.e., a late Double Time declaration extends the AFV's Armored Assault movement by exactly the one hex the squad could now afford, and the AFV may continue without the squad, since the limit is on how far the Infantry *could* have accompanied it, not on where the Infantry actually ends up.

Points to note:
- Timing: the old requirement that Double Time be declared at the start of the MPh has been rewritten — a unit may declare it any time during its MPh, but if it waits until after expending MF it gets only a 1 MF bonus while still taking the full CX penalties (A4.5).
- The squad is marked CX and suffers the normal CX penalties (+1 to attack/labor DR, +1 to CC DR made, -1 to CC attacks against it, etc.) and its CX counter also prohibits Double Time next MPh (A4.51).
- Double Time is legal here because the squad never mounts/rides a conveyance — Armored Assault Infantry move on foot beneath the AFV (A4.5 prohibits DT only for units that will mount/ride/dismount a conveyance that Player Turn).
- The AFV gains one additional hex only if that hex costs 1 MF to the Infantry (Open Ground here, so yes).

References:
- (A4.5) Double Time — may be declared any time during the MPh; declared late it grants only one extra MF, with normal CX penalties
- (D9.31) Armored Assault — the AFV "cannot move farther than if it were accompanied by that same Infantry throughout the move — even if that Infantry fails to end its MPh in the same hex"; the rule's example explicitly allows the AFV one more hex on a late Double Time
- (A4.51) Counter Exhaustion — CX penalties and removal timing; CX prohibits Double Time in the unit's next MPh
- (A4.2) Base Infantry MF allotment (4 MF) that the Double Time bonus modifies
results.llm_reasoning: [Calculation] The model's answer matches the expected answer: it correctly states that a squad may declare a late Double Time while remaining in its hex, that a late declaration grants only one extra MF (not two), that the infantry need not actually spend that MF (declaring is sufficient), that the squad becomes CX and suffers the normal CX penalties, and that the AFV may move one additional open-ground hex but not beyond what the accompanying infantry could have done. The response cites the relevant rules (A4.5, A4.51, D9.31) and the D9.31 example, aligning with the expected ruling.

results.question:
In Close Combat where two enemy infantry units are in the same location with a single AFV and infantry attack sequentially (attacker first):
1) After the first infantry attack fails to eliminate the AFV, may the AFV choose which of the two infantry units to attack (including the unit that has not yet attacked)?
2) If the AFV attacks and eliminates the infantry unit that has not yet made its attack, does that eliminated unit still get to make its CC attack back?
3) If after the sequence (infantry 1 attacks, AFV attacks, infantry 2 attacks) there are still no results, can the AFV then make a second CC attack if it used all its CC weapons in its initial attack?
results.expected_answer: Close Combat involving a vehicle is sequential, not simultaneous. (1) After the first infantry attack fails, the AFV may attack and may choose which eligible enemy unit(s) to attack—including the unit that has not yet attacked—but only up to the number of CC attacks its weapons allow. (2) If the AFV eliminates an infantry unit before that unit has attacked, the eliminated unit does NOT get to attack back (this is why CC attacks are rolled in the correct order). (3) The AFV gets no extra attack after the sequence unless it actually has multiple separate CC attacks (e.g., a CMG plus an sN). On the Nahverteidigungswaffe specifically (A11.622): the sN may be used only during the CCPh after the AFV (or its Personnel escort) has been attacked that CCPh—units that never attack the AFV are simply not subject to it—and when the sN fires it attacks all vulnerable units in the Location.
results.model_response:
**Answer:** 1) Yes — when its turn in the attack sequence comes, the AFV may attack any enemy unit(s) in the Location, including the infantry unit that has not yet attacked; sequential attacks need not be predesignated (A11.3, A11.31). 2) No — a unit that is eliminated before it attacks forfeits its attack opportunity (A11.3). 3) No — each side alternates attacks one at a time until all units have attacked, been eliminated, or passed (A11.31), so an AFV that already combined all of its usable CC weapons/FP into its one attack has no further attack that CCPh.

---

1) A11.31 requires all CC attacks in a Location containing a vehicle to be declared sequentially: the non-vehicular player attacks first with one attack, and thereafter the sides alternate attacks one at a time. Nothing restricts the AFV's target choice to units that have already attacked, and A11.3 expressly allows each player to observe the results of each attack before declaring the next one — so the AFV may deliberately target the infantry unit that has not yet attacked.

2) A11.3 states that in sequential CC "any unit which is eliminated before it attacks forfeits its attack opportunity." An infantry unit eliminated by the AFV's attack before making its own attack therefore gets no CC attack back.

3) The alternation of A11.31 continues only "until all units in that Location have attacked, been eliminated, or passed." Each unit gets its attack opportunity once per CCPh. An AFV may split its armament (CMG, RMG, AAMG, etc.) into separate CC attacks rather than one combined attack (A11.62) — and an sN Close Defense Weapon System attack can never be combined with other FP (A11.622) — but if the AFV already expended all of its CC weapons in its initial attack, it cannot make a second attack in that CCPh just because no result was achieved. The Location is then in Melee for resolution in a subsequent CCPh (assuming the AFV can be held in Melee).

References:
- (A11.3) SEQUENTIAL CC — attacks are resolved in order; a unit eliminated before it attacks forfeits its attack; sequential attacks need not be predesignated
- (A11.31) vs VEHICLE — non-vehicular player attacks first with one attack; sides then alternate one attack at a time until all units have attacked, been eliminated, or passed
- (A11.62) AFV CC armament — an AFV may combine its CMG/RMG/AAMG/Passenger FP into one or more combined attacks or use them separately
- (A11.622) Close Defense Weapon System — an sN attack cannot be combined with any other form of FP
results.llm_reasoning: [Explanation] The model's response correctly states the core points: CC is sequential; the AFV may choose which enemy infantry to attack (including a unit that has not yet attacked); a unit eliminated before its attack forfeits that attack; and the AFV cannot make an extra attack unless it actually has multiple separate CC attacks (e.g., separate weapons/FP). However, it omits an important nuance from the expected answer regarding the Nahverteidigungswaffe (sN): A11.622 limits the sN so it may be used only after the AFV (or its escort) has been attacked that CCPh and, when used, it affects all vulnerable units in the Location. The model only noted that the sN cannot be combined with other FP, but did not state the conditional timing/targeting restriction, which is a key concept in the expected answer.
results.human_notes: No mention of sN in the question.

results.question: A10.51 lets a routing unit ignore hexes of the building it is currently in as routing targets. Does the same exception apply to woods (woodland patches)? If the woods patch is a single hex, can a routing unit route away from that woods hex (i.e., rout through/leave that woods hex)?
results.expected_answer: No—the A10.51 'may ignore' allowance is for the building you are in, not for woods. A unit may still attempt to leave the hex it begins the RtPh in, but a woods patch is not auto-ignorable as a destination the way a building is. A unit under DM that starts in a woods/building can still rout if otherwise allowed; it may rout THROUGH a single-hex woods (i.e., not one it starts the RtPh in) when that hex is ignorable / not its target, or if the hex becomes untenable (e.g., an enemy becomes Known ADJACENT), or if it is routing to an ADJACENT building. It may also continue routing through consecutive woods/building hexes until it runs out of MF if all other criteria are met; otherwise, once it reaches its target it must stop.
results.model_response:
**Answer:** Yes — the same treatment applies to woods. Official Q&A on A10.51 confirms that a broken unit beginning its RtPh in a woods hex is NOT required to designate its own hex as its rout target just because it is the "nearest" woods hex at zero MF; it may rout away from that woods hex toward another building/woods hex.

---

The A10.51 exception is worded in terms of buildings: "A broken unit in a building need not consider a hex of the same building in which it begins the RtPh (or when a new destination is re-figured) as its closest building hex if it prefers to rout out of that building altogether and toward another building/woods hex — even if it must cross Open Ground or another building hex of the same building to do so."

For woods, the official Q&A resolves the question directly: "A broken unit begins its RtPh DM in a woods hex with no ADJACENT woods/building hexes. There are no KEU in LOS. Since the nearest woods hex is its own at zero MF, must it choose its own hex as its target? A. No." [K34]

So a unit in a single-hex woods patch may rout away from (out of) that woods hex and head toward another building/woods hex instead. The other A10.51 exception also applies equally to woods by its own text: a routing unit may ignore any building/woods hex that is no farther from a Known enemy unit than its starting hex, even if it must rout through that ignored hex to reach its destination. All other rout restrictions (may not move closer to a Known enemy unit in LOS, must stop upon reaching a non-ADJACENT-to-enemy building/woods Location, etc.) still apply along the way.

References:
- (A10.51) Rout destination — must move to nearest building/woods hex; EXC allows ignoring hexes of the unit's own starting building, and ignoring any building/woods hex no farther from a Known enemy unit than the starting hex
- (A10.51 Q&A [K34]) A unit starting its RtPh in a woods hex need not choose its own woods hex as its rout target
results.llm_reasoning: [Explanation] The model's answer contradicts the expected answer. The expected answer emphasizes that the A10.51 'may ignore' allowance is worded for the building a unit is in and is not an automatic 'ignore' for woods; although a routing unit can still leave the woods hex it starts in under certain conditions, woods are not treated the same as the building exception. The model asserts the exception 'applies to woods' and cites Q&A K34 as supporting that claim, which conflicts with the expected explanation. Because the core conclusion (whether the A10.51 building-ignore exception equally applies to woods) differs from the expected answer, the model response is incorrect.
results.human_notes: I think the answer is correct.

results.question: Under A23.4 and A23.6, when a unit throws a demolition charge (thrown DC) and the throwing unit is CX, does the +1 DRM for CX apply to both the DR for the Target Location and the DR for the Thrower's Location, or only to the Target Location?
results.expected_answer: It applies to both Locations. A23.4 adds the +1 CX DRM to the Attack Resolution DR (or Position DR; C7.346), and a thrown DC makes a separate attack on the Target Location (+2 DRM) and on the Thrower's Location (+3 DRM) per A23.6, each resolved with its own DR. The CX +1 modifies each of those DRs—just as the per-Location TEM is applied to each. (This is the consensus interpretation by experienced players; it has not been set by an official Q&A.)
results.model_response:
**Answer:** The +1 CX DRM applies to both DRs. A23.4 states that if the Placing/Throwing unit is CX, the +1 DRM is applied to "the Attack Resolution DR" — and for a Thrown DC there is a separate Attack Resolution DR for each Location attacked (A23.6), so both the DR vs the Target Location and the DR vs the Thrower's Location take the +1 CX DRM.

---

A23.6 makes a Thrown DC attack both Locations: "both the target's and thrower's Locations are immediately attacked by the DC," with the target Location taking the +2 (+3) Thrown-DC DRM and the Thrower's Location a +3 (+4) DRM, and "a separate DR is used to resolve the attack of a Thrown DC on each Location."

A23.4 (DETONATION) then says: "If the Placing/Throwing unit is CX, the +1 DRM for CX attack is applied to the Attack Resolution DR (or Position DR; C7.346)." Read literally, this attaches the CX penalty to the resolution of the DC's attack, not to one particular Location — and since each Location's resolution is an Attack Resolution DR of that CX unit's DC attack, the +1 is added to each. So a CX unit throwing a DC resolves the target Location at +2 (Thrown) +1 (CX) plus TEM, and its own Location at +3 (Thrower) +1 (CX) plus TEM.

Note the AFV exception: when the DC is Thrown at an AFV, the CX +1 (like the Thrown +2/+3) is applied to the special DC Position DR instead of the IFT resolution (A23.4, C7.346).

References:
- (A23.4) DETONATION — the CX +1 DRM is applied to the Attack Resolution DR (or Position DR; C7.346)
- (A23.6) THROWN DC — both target and thrower Locations are attacked, +2/+3 vs target and +3/+4 vs thrower, with a separate DR resolving each Location
- (C7.346) DC vs AFV — Placed/Thrown DC vs an armored target uses a special DC Position DR, which is where the Thrown/CX DRM apply instead
results.llm_reasoning: [Explanation] The model's answer matches the expected answer: it correctly states that the +1 CX DRM applies to both the Target Location DR and the Thrower's Location DR. It cites A23.4 applying CX to the Attack Resolution/Position DR, notes A23.6 creates separate DRs for each Location, and therefore the CX +1 modifies each DR. The model also correctly mentions the AFV Position DR exception, which is additional correct context.

results.question: Can a unit that is on top of a Wire counter control that hex or Location for Victory Conditions?
results.expected_answer: Yes. Being on top of a Wire counter does not by itself prevent a unit from gaining Control of that hex/Location for Victory Condition purposes. B26.32 treats units on and under Wire as being in the same Location, and nothing in A26.11/A26.13 bars Control merely because the unit is on the Wire (the unit must still otherwise qualify for Control—e.g., be the only side with an armed Good Order unit present, at ground level).
results.model_response:
**Answer:** Yes. A unit on top of a Wire counter still occupies that Location/hex, so an armed Good Order Infantry MMC on the Wire can gain Control of it per A26.11. The list of things Infantry on top of a Wire counter are prohibited from doing (B26.32) does not include gaining Control.

---

Being on top of the Wire does not put the unit somewhere else: a unit on a Wire counter and one beneath the Wire counter in the same hex are in the same Location (B26.1). Control is gained during play by occupying the Location/hex/building with an armed Good Order Infantry MMC without an armed enemy ground unit present there (A26.11); the only occupation-based method that is expressly barred is Bypass ("Control cannot be gained via Bypass"). Wire is not Bypass — it is simply a Fortification counter the unit sits on top of.

While on top of a Wire counter, Infantry are restricted from certain activities — they cannot Search, create an entrenchment, Recover/Transfer a SW/Gun beneath that Wire counter, or enter a pillbox/vehicle counter in that hex (B26.32) — but gaining Control is not among the prohibited actions. (They also suffer +1 to their own attacks and are at a disadvantage in CC; B26.31.)

References:
- (A26.11) Gaining Control — occupying a Location/hex/building with an armed Good Order Infantry MMC (with no armed enemy ground unit present) gains Control; only Bypass is excluded as a means of gaining Control
- (B26.1) Wire — Wire is a Fortification counter; a unit on a Wire counter and one beneath it in the same hex are in the same Location, so the unit on the Wire occupies that Location
- (B26.32) Restrictions on Wire — Infantry on top of a Wire counter cannot Search, entrench, Recover/Transfer a SW/Gun beneath the Wire, or enter a pillbox/vehicle in the hex; gaining Control is not listed as prohibited
results.llm_reasoning: [Calculation] The model's response matches the expected answer. It gives the correct final value (Yes) and cites the correct rule logic: a unit on a Wire counter is in the same Location/hex for Control purposes and nothing in the Wire restrictions forbids gaining Control (the unit still must otherwise qualify per A26.11). The response also correctly lists the actual prohibitions on units atop Wire, without asserting any incorrect additional restrictions.

results.question: In the Movement Phase a moving 7-0 leader enters a hex containing a 9-1 leader and two squads. A Fire Attack against the moving 7-0 causes a MC; the 7-0 rolls HOB and becomes berserk. The 9-1 then takes its TC, passes, and becomes berserk as well. Are the two squads in the hex subject to two Terror Checks (one from each berserk leader)? Is one of those TCs at -1 DRM?
results.expected_answer: Yes—each squad is subject to a Berserk-conversion TC from each berserk leader (two TCs apiece), and one of them carries a -1 DRM. Per A15.41, when a leader goes berserk he must (after first resolving his fire attack) attempt to turn the other friendly units in his Location berserk 'even if … not subject to that same attack'; each such unit takes a Berserk NTC 'subject to the berserk leader's modification', after which that leader forfeits his leadership DRM. So the 7-0's conversion attempt is made at its 0 modifier and the (now-berserk) 9-1's conversion attempt is made at -1. Leader checks follow A10.2 order (higher-Morale leaders first), and the 7-0—as the only moving unit—takes its original MC first per A10.21. Net: the two squads each face two Berserk TCs, one at 0 (from the 7-0) and one at -1 (from the 9-1).
results.model_response:
**Answer:** Yes to both. Each leader who goes berserk must attempt to change all other friendly units in his Location to berserk (A15.41), so the two squads take two separate Berserk (Terror) Checks — one triggered by the berserk 7-0 (0 DRM) and one triggered by the berserk 9-1, which is taken at the 9-1's -1 leadership DRM.

---

A15.41 (LEADER CONSEQUENCES): "When a leader goes berserk, he must (after first resolving any fire attack vs any other targets in that Location) attempt to change any other friendly units (even if broken or not subject to that same attack) in the same Location to berserk status also. Each such unit must take a NTC subject to the berserk leader's modification. If they pass the Berserk TC they become berserk; if not, there is no change to their status. After the Berserk TC, the leader forfeits his leadership DRM until he returns to normal."

Applying this to the scenario:
- The 7-0 goes berserk via HOB (A15.4). It does not matter that the 9-1 and the squads were not part of the attack — A15.41 applies "even if... not subject to that same attack." The 9-1 and both squads each take a Berserk TC with the 7-0's leadership modification (0 DRM).
- The 9-1 passes and becomes berserk. Official Q&A confirms a leader who involuntarily becomes berserk always attempts to berserk the other units in his Location, regardless of how he went berserk [VFTT 27 Feb 1999]. So the two squads (assuming they did not already pass the first TC and go berserk) take a second Berserk TC.
- This second TC is "subject to the berserk leader's modification" — the 9-1's -1 DRM — since he only forfeits his leadership DRM after administering the Berserk TC.

Any squad that passes either TC becomes berserk (and is not checked again); failing a Berserk TC causes no status change (A15.41).

References:
- (A15.4) BERSERK — created by a Final Heat of Battle DR of 9-11; a broken unit that goes berserk is automatically rallied
- (A15.41) LEADER CONSEQUENCES — each berserk leader forces a Berserk TC on all other friendly units in his Location, modified by that leader's DRM; the leader forfeits his DRM only after the TC
- (A15.41 Q&A [VFTT 27 Feb 1999]) — a leader who involuntarily becomes berserk always attempts to change the other units in his Location, not only when berserk resulted from a fire attack
results.llm_reasoning: [Explanation] The model's answer correctly states that each squad must make two separate Berserk (Terror) Checks — one from the berserk 7-0 at 0 DRM and one from the berserk 9-1 at -1 DRM — and it correctly cites A15.41 (leader-administered conversion checks) and the rule that a leader's DRM applies to the check and is forfeited only after administering it. These are the central points in the expected answer. The model omitted explicit citation of A10.2/A10.21 ordering language, but it nonetheless presents the correct sequence (7-0's conversion attempts first, then the 9-1's) and the correct final result, so its explanation matches the expected outcome.

results.question: A4.7 says a unit may not change both its location within a hex and also the hex it is in during the same APh. Since a pillbox is a separate location, can a unit advance into an adjacent hex and enter the pillbox in the same APh?
results.expected_answer: No, with one exception. A4.7 forbids changing both your Location within a hex and the hex you are in during the same APh, so a unit normally cannot advance into an adjacent hex and also enter a pillbox (a separate Location) in that same APh—the same reason you cannot advance into a building hex and then move to its Level 1 or cellar. Exception: if the pillbox is a bunker (B30.8), a unit in a Location containing a connecting trench may advance into the bunker/pillbox directly from an adjacent hex.
results.model_response:
**Answer:** No. Because a pillbox is a separate Location in its hex, advancing into the adjacent hex and then entering the pillbox would be changing both the hex and the Location within the hex — exactly what A4.7 prohibits in the same APh. The advancing unit ends its APh in the pillbox hex outside the pillbox; it may enter the pillbox Location in a later phase (the only shortcuts are a tunnel or a bunker connection).

---

A4.7: a unit "may use the APh to move one hex horizontally or vertically... but not both. A unit may not change both its Location within a hex and also the hex it is in during the same APh." A pillbox is a separate Location from the rest of its hex (B30.34), so entering it is a change of Location within the hex and cannot be combined with the one-hex advance. The elevation allowance in A4.7 (advancing to an adjacent hex of different elevation, e.g., hill or Depression) does not help here, and the entrenchment allowance ("from inside an entrenchment to inside another in an Accessible hex") is an explicit exception for entrenchments only — a pillbox is not an entrenchment and has its own entry procedure.

This matches the MPh procedure: entering or exiting a pillbox Location is a separate action costing one MF that "cannot be combined with any other MF expenditure" (B30.33, B30.4) — a unit first enters the pillbox hex, then separately enters the pillbox.

The stated exceptions for entering a pillbox Location directly from elsewhere are a tunnel (B8.6; see the RtPh/APh tunnel-entrance EXC in B30.42) and a bunker (B30.8; see the EXC in B30.4). Absent those, the advancing unit stops in the pillbox hex outside the pillbox. Note also B30.42: it could not enter the pillbox Location at all while an armed enemy ground unit is in the hex outside the pillbox, and B30.44: it may never enter a pillbox containing enemy Infantry.

References:
- (A4.7) ADVANCE PHASE — one hex OR a Location change within the hex, never both, in the same APh
- (B30.34) A pillbox is a separate Location from the rest of its hex
- (B30.4 / B30.33) Entering/exiting a pillbox Location is a separate one-MF action that cannot be combined with other MF expenditure [EXC: tunnel (B8.6); bunker (B30.8)]
- (B30.42) No move/rout/advance/Withdraw into a pillbox Location while an enemy ground unit is in the hex outside it [EXC: RtPh/APh entrance via tunnel]
results.llm_reasoning: [Explanation] The model's answer matches the expected answer: it correctly states that A4.7 forbids changing both hex and Location in the same APh (so you cannot advance into a hex and enter a pillbox there in the same APh) and it identifies the bunker connection exception (B30.8). The model also mentions the tunnel/bunker exceptions and cites relevant rules (B30.x, B8.6), which is additional correct context and does not contradict the expected answer. Minor wording differences (and an extra note about tunnel exceptions) do not change the semantic correctness.

results.question: If a carrier infantry stand sets up outside its vehicle and the vehicle is abandoned/scrounged at scenario start, is that vehicle treated as a wreck or can it be concealed at the scenario start?
results.expected_answer: It is not a wreck, and it can be concealed only under the normal rules. A vehicle may set up Abandoned only if a Special Scenario Rule allows it (Carriers are not special in this regard); an Abandoned vehicle uses an Abandoned counter and is NOT a wreck. Per the A12.2 Q&A, a concealed vehicle that becomes Abandoned may retain its '?' (if otherwise allowed), but once it loses concealment it cannot regain a '?' while Abandoned. So if an SSR permits abandoned setup, the scrounged Carrier at scenario start is an Abandoned vehicle that may be set up concealed—it is not treated as a wreck.
results.model_response:
**Answer:** First, note that this setup is generally not legal: per official Q&A on D5.4, a vehicle may start a scenario with its crew/inherent Infantry outside it (or with its weapons Scrounged) "only in certain CG." Where such a setup is permitted, the abandoned/scrounged vehicle is NOT a wreck — a wreck only exists when a vehicle has been eliminated — so it remains a vehicle and, like any concealment-eligible vehicle, it may set up concealed under A12.2 (in Concealment Terrain/out of enemy LOS).

---

References:
- (D5.4) Official Q&A: "Can a vehicle start a scenario with its crew outside the AFV or with any of its weapons Scrounged/removed? ... A. Only in certain CG. No." — the baseline legality of this setup
- (D10.5) Scrounging is performed on "a non-burning wreck or abandoned vehicle" — the rules explicitly distinguish an abandoned vehicle from a wreck; a Scrounged counter marks it as scrounged but does not turn it into a wreck
- (A12.2) Vehicle concealment — a concealed vehicle's BU/CE/TCA status may be secretly recorded at setup; related Q&A confirms a vehicle "would start Immobilized only by SSR," i.e., an on-board vehicle sets up as a vehicle (concealable), not as a wreck
- (B25.14) Wreck Blaze / wreck status arises from a vehicle being eliminated — abandonment alone never creates a wreck
results.llm_reasoning: [Explanation] The model's answer matches the expected answer: it correctly states the vehicle is not a wreck, that abandonment/scrounging at scenario start is only allowed if an SSR/CSG permits it, and that an Abandoned vehicle is treated as a vehicle (and may be concealed only under the normal concealment rules, per A12.2). The response also correctly distinguishes scrounged/abandoned counters from wrecks (citing D10.5 and that elimination creates a wreck). The model omitted a minor detail about the Q&A on retaining a '?' while becoming Abandoned, but that is extra nuance and does not change the core correctness.

results.question: If an infantry squad conducts an Assault Movement into a hex and then spends additional MF there to place a Demolition Charge (DC), do the MF expended to place the DC count as part of the Assault Movement (i.e., remain under the Assault Movement rules) or not?
results.expected_answer: Yes—the MF used to place the DC remain part of the Assault Movement. A unit may not "partially" Assault Move: it must declare AM before it starts moving and, once declared, AM cannot be voluntarily voided (A4.61). Additional MF spent within a Location (placing a DC, or a grenade/Smoke attempt) on top of a one-Location move do not break AM, provided the unit still moves ≤ one Location and does not expend all of its MF. E.g., AM into a grain hex for 1.5 MF plus a 2-MF DC placement = 3.5 MF total is still a legal Assault Move for a 4-MF squad (see the A23.3 example).
results.model_response:
**Answer:** Yes — the MF spent to place the DC remain part of (and do not void) the Assault Movement, because additional MF expenditure within a Location, in addition to the one change of Location, does not negate Assault Movement (A4.61). The exception: if in placing the DC the squad expends all of its MF (or gains CX status), it is no longer considered to be using Assault Movement and is subject to the -1 FFNAM DRM for the remainder of its MPh.

---

Detail: Assault Movement requires the Infantry unit to move ≤ one Location during its MPh without using all of its MF (A4.61). A4.61 expressly allows "additional MF expenditure within a Location ... in addition to a change of Location" without voiding Assault Movement "unless, in so doing, a unit expends all its MF or [gains] CX status." DC placement is such an in-Location MF expenditure (A23.3). Note also that official Q&A confirms a concealed unit may Place a DC while using Assault Movement — it simply loses concealment for that placement activity (A12.14/A12.141 Q&A, Concealment Table Case C).

References:
- (A4.61) Assault Movement — additional MF expenditure within a Location does not negate Assault Movement unless it expends all the unit's MF or causes CX; a unit that ends up not qualifying is subject to -1 FFNAM for the rest of its MPh
- (A23.3) DC Placement — placement is an MF expenditure by the carrying Infantry unit in/adjacent to its Location
- (A12.14/A12.141) Official Q&A — a concealed unit using Assault Movement to Place a DC loses concealment for that activity (confirming DC placement is legal during Assault Movement)
results.llm_reasoning: [Explanation] The model's answer matches the expected answer: it states that MF spent to place a DC remain part of the Assault Movement, correctly cites A4.61 that additional in-Location MF does not void AM, notes the exceptions (expending all MF or becoming CX), references A23.3 that DC placement is an in-Location MF expenditure, and mentions the Q&A about concealment. All key concepts from the expected answer are present and accurately represented.

results.question: If an infantry unit attempts to enter a hex that contains a hidden AP minefield (and a concealed enemy MMC) but is denied entry (e.g., forced to remain in its previous hex), is the moving infantry unit subject to a mine attack from the target hex? Does the answer change if the entry is denied because the destination is a fortified building?
results.expected_answer: No—in both cases the unit is not subject to the mine attack, because it never actually enters the mined hex. A12.15 is explicit: "A unit forced back to its previous Location is not subject to attack (even by a minefield or FFE) during its brief period in the previously-concealed unit's Location." When entry is instead denied because the destination is a Fortified Building Location occupied by an enemy squad, the unit "must remain in its present hex" (B23.922) and is likewise not attacked by the A-P minefield (confirmed by the B23.922 Q&A). It expends the MF but takes no mine attack.
results.model_response:
**Answer:** No. A unit forced back by a concealed defender is explicitly *not* subject to attack — "even by a minefield or FFE" — during its brief stay in the previously-concealed unit's Location (A12.15), so the hidden AP minefield in the target hex does not attack it. The answer is the same (still no mine attack) if entry is denied by a Fortified Building: per B23.922 the unit never enters the target Location at all — its MF are considered expended in its *present* hex — so the entry trigger for a minefield attack (B28.41) never occurs.

---

Two important caveats:

1. **The unit's own hex matters.** While the target hex's mines do not attack, a unit forced back into a Location that itself contains a minefield or FFE *is* subject to that minefield/FFE attack as it re-enters, and any already-existing Residual FP in the re-entered Location attacks it — even if that Residual FP already attacked it that phase (A12.15).
2. **Fortified Building case.** An Infantry unit attempting to move into a Fortified Building Location occupied by an unpinned, Good Order, armed enemy squad-equivalent loses that MPh capability and remains in its present hex; the MF expended (not lost) count as expended in its present hex for Residual FP, Defensive First Fire, Subsequent First Fire, and FPF purposes, and once that fire is completed its MPh is over (B23.922). Since it never enters the fortified (mined) Location, no entry/exit of the minefield hex occurs, so no mine attack is announced (B28.41). Loss-of-concealment rules (A12.15) still apply even though the Location cannot be entered.

References:
- (A12.15) Detection — a unit forced back to its previous Location "is not subject to attack (even by a minefield or FFE) during its brief stay in the previously-concealed unit's Location"; but re-entry into a minefield/FFE/Residual FP Location does trigger those attacks
- (B23.922) Fortified Building Entry — failed entry attempt: unit remains in its present hex, MF considered expended there for Residual FP/Defensive First Fire purposes; concealment-loss rules still apply
- (B28.41) Minefield Attacks — a minefield attack is announced whenever a unit enters/leaves a minefield hex; denied entry means no entry occurred
results.llm_reasoning: [Explanation] The model's answer matches the expected answer: in both cases the moving infantry is not subject to the mine attack because it never actually enters the mined hex. The response correctly cites A12.15 and B23.922, notes that the minefield is expended without an attack, and references B28.41 for the entry trigger. The added caveats about re-entering a mined/FFE Location and the Fortified Building MPh effects are accurate and do not contradict the expected answer.

results.question: If a unit declares Assault Movement and attempts to enter a concealed enemy-occupied location but is bumped back by detection (A12.15), is it still considered to be using Assault Movement for any defensive fire, and how are the movement factors expended treated? Does it matter if the location attempted is Fortified?
results.expected_answer: Yes—it is still using Assault Movement. A12.15 states the MF spent attempting to enter the concealed unit's Location are instead considered expended in the Location it is returned to, and nothing terminates Assault Movement (a unit cannot partially Assault Move). So the bumped-back unit may be fired on in the Location it is returned to, but because it is Assault Moving (and did not enter a new hex) FFNAM does not apply—"bump-scouting" under AM is in fact a common way to avoid FFNAM. Denial of entry to a Fortified Building Location works the same way. (This is the consensus reading of A12.15; no rule states that AM ends.)
results.model_response:
**Answer:** Yes — the bumped unit is still using Assault Movement (once declared, Assault Movement is only negated by expending all MF, gaining CX, or breaking/going berserk; A4.61), so Defensive First Fire against it does not get the -1 FFNAM DRM. The MF expended in the failed entry attempt are still used, but are considered expended in the Location the unit is forced back to (A12.15) — it ends its MPh there, loses concealment, and is subject to Defensive First Fire (with the TEM of the Location it never left, per Q&A). A Fortified Building Location works essentially the same way: the A12.15 concealment/detection rules apply even though the Location can never be entered, and the MF expended (not lost) in the unsuccessful attempt are likewise considered expended in the unit's present hex for Residual FP, Defensive First Fire, Subsequent First Fire, and FPF — the added penalty is that the unit loses its MPh (or APh) capability for that turn (B23.922).

---

Detail:
- Bump mechanics (A12.15): when a unit attempts to move into a concealed enemy Location, the DEFENDER reveals at least one concealed unit and forces the mover back to the last Location it occupied, where it loses concealment, ends its MPh (unless it goes berserk), and is subject to Defensive First Fire. It is not subject to attack during its "brief stay" in the concealed unit's Location (no Snap Shot from the firer's hex on the attempted entry), but it is subject to Snap Shots/other First Fire while being returned.
- MF treatment (A12.15): "The MF expended in attempting to move into the concealed unit's Location are still used, but are considered expended in the Location it is returned to" — so already-existing Residual FP, FFE, or minefields in the returned-to Location attack it on re-entry (a unit forced back to an entrenchment/shellhole gets no TEM from it vs the ensuing Defensive First Fire). Official Q&A confirms the fired-on unit receives the TEM of the Location it did not leave (e.g., +2 for the trench it was bounced back into).
- Assault Movement status (A4.61): additional/failed MF expenditure does not void a declared Assault Movement unless the unit thereby expends all its MF or gains CX (or breaks/goes berserk from Defensive First Fire) — so the -1 FFNAM does not apply to fire on the bumped unit, though FFMO could still apply if it is in Open Ground.
- Fortified Location (B23.922): entry is simply impossible while an unpinned, Good Order, armed enemy squad-equivalent is inside; the attempting unit "loses that MPh or APh capability for that turn," and the MF expended (not lost) are considered expended in its present hex for Residual FP (A12.15), Defensive First Fire, Subsequent First Fire, or FPF; once such fire is completed its MPh is over. "Loss of concealment rules (A12.15) apply even though the Fortified Location cannot be entered."

References:
- (A12.15) Detection — bump-back procedure, MF considered expended in the returned-to Location, loss of concealment and end of MPh
- (A4.61) Assault Movement — declared AM is voided only by expending all MF, CX, or breaking/berserk, so the bumped unit is still Assault Moving (no FFNAM)
- (B23.922) Fortified Building entry — failed attempt costs the MPh/APh for the turn; MF treated as expended in the present hex; A12.15 concealment loss still applies

results.question: To inflict Desperation Morale (or qualify for Encirclement), an attack must have "enough FP (taking the possibility of Cowering into account) to possibly inflict at least an NMC." How does the possibility of Cowering factor in — e.g., does a small attack that would Cower below NMC capability still cause DM?
results.expected_answer: It depends on whether the firer can Cower. The test is whether the attack could possibly inflict at least an NMC; if the firer is subject to Cowering you must evaluate it after a cowering reduction—so a small attack that would Cower below NMC capability does NOT cause DM. But if the firer cannot Cower—a SMC, leader-directed fire, ordnance, or a Cowering-immune Elite/1st-Line British unit (A25.45)—no reduction is possible, so even a 1-FP attack with a +1 DRM that could yield an NMC does inflict DM and can enable Encirclement (A7.7). In short: if, accounting for this firer's actual Cowering possibility, the attack could still inflict ≥ NMC, the broken unit becomes DM.
results.model_response:
**Answer:** "Taking the possibility of Cowering into account" means you must assume the worst case: if the attack is capable of Cowering, evaluate it as if it had Cowered (i.e., after the leftward column shift), and it must still be able to possibly inflict at least an NMC on the target (best possible DR, after all applicable DRM). So a small attack that, had it Cowered, could no longer possibly yield an NMC does NOT qualify to inflict DM (A10.62) or to count as valid Encircling fire (A7.7) — regardless of what the actual DR turns out to be. Attacks that cannot Cower (e.g., leader-directed fire, or ordnance, which is immune to Cowering per A7.9) are evaluated at their full FP/column.

---

Detail:
- A10.62 (DM): an already-broken unit becomes DM when attacked by CC/WP "or enough FP (taking the possibility of Cowering into account) to possibly inflict at least a NMC result on the target." The test is about capability, not the actual result — but the capability is judged assuming the Cowering shift where Cowering is possible.
- A7.7 (Encirclement) uses the identical standard for valid fire: "ordnance weapons must secure a hit on the target, and other firers must exert enough FP (taking the possibility of Cowering into account) to possibly inflict at least a NMC result." Related Q&A confirms the threshold is applied to the qualifying fire itself — e.g., a FG mixing normal-range and long-range firers does not qualify if the normal-range units by themselves lack the strength to possibly cause a MC (A7.7 Q&A, VFTT 18 Sep 1998).
- The "possibility" framing also appears in the Dust Q&A (A7.7, A10.62, F11.71-.72): you do not roll the actual Dust dr — "just the possibility of the lowest Dust DLV is considered" when determining whether enough FP was applied. Cowering, by the explicit rule text, is the pessimistic side of the same test: the attack must clear the NMC bar even in its Cowered state.

References:
- (A10.62) Desperation Morale — FP threshold "taking the possibility of Cowering into account"
- (A7.7) Encirclement — identical valid-fire standard; ordnance must hit, other fire must possibly inflict an NMC after allowing for Cowering
- (A7.9) Ordnance/leader-directed attacks immune to Cowering are counted at full effect
- (A7.7/A10.62/F11.71-.72 Q&A) "Possibility" is evaluated without rolling the variable dr (Dust example)

Second, a leader can only direct units in his own Location (A7.531), so he cannot direct — and therefore cannot protect from Cowering — the Fire Group units in the other Locations. Only the units in his Location are leader-directed ("no one cowers when directed by a leader"); the undirected units in the other Locations remain subject to Cowering, so an Original Doubles DR still Cowers the attack, with Random Selection applied only among the units subject to Cowering.

- (A7.53-.531 & A10.72 Q&A) "These seem to imply that leader direction and the application of a leader's DRM can be declared independently of each other. Is this true? A. No; the two are synonymous in all cases." So the premise of withholding the DRM while still "directing" is not legal. (A 0-DRM leader can of course direct to prevent Cowering, since his DRM simply has no numeric effect.)
- (A7.9) Cowering and mixed FGs: per Q&A, when a FG contains units immune to Cowering and units that are not, an Original Doubles still Cowers the attack (1 column shift; 2 for Inexperienced), and Random Selection for the Final Fire marking is applied only among the units subject to Cowering. The same logic governs a multi-Location FG where only one Location's units are leader-directed.

- (A7.531) Leader must be in the same Location as the units he directs

results.question: May a dug-in, non-turreted AFV change its Vehicle CA?

Since a non-turreted AFV has no turret, its Covered Arc is fixed in the direction it was dug in; it cannot pivot to bring its armament to bear elsewhere.

- (D9.5) ARMORED CUPOLA — equivalent of an Immobile tank; may change TCA 360 degrees but cannot leave its hex

- (D3.12) TURRET COVERED ARC — TCA governs turret-mounted weapons only; a non-turreted AFV's Field of Fire is its VCA

results.question: Must an AFV fire all of its weapons before another unit fires, and may it fire its MGs, let another unit fire, then fire its MA later in the phase?

- (D3.51) MAINTAINING CA — once any vehicular weapon fires, remaining weapons fire only from that same hex and pay the established CA-change penalty
- (A.15) Fire phase definition referenced by D3.5
